coredata.dc:title: Modulation effect of carbon quantum dots in organic electroluminescent devices
coredata.dc:creator: Qian, Kun
Li, Hairong
Zheng, Bo
Chang, Fangzhi
Lei, Qi
Han, Genliang
Song, Yuzhe
Liu, Su
Wei, Yongzhi
coredata.dc:description: In this work, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are embedded as a functional material into white-organic-light-emitting diodes (WOLEDs) devices with a structure of dual-light-emitting layer. When the forward voltage is applied on devices ranging from 14 V to 22 V, the CIE coordinates of CQDs WOLEDs changed from (0.27,0.29) to (0.34,0.37) which exhibits linear optical modulation effect. Meanwhile the as-fabricated CQDs WOLEDs obtain excellent optical and electrical properties. By embedding CQDs, WOLEDs with a low turn-on voltage of 12 V which is 40% lower than reference devices can be achieved. Moreover, the devices could also obtain high luminous brightness with a lower voltage. Such superior properties are attributed to the CQDs multiple exciton generation (MEG) effect. The results demonstrate that CQDs as functional materials of WOLEDs may open up new thoughts to develop the optically modifiable WOLEDs and the low power consumption optoelectronic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Organic light-emitting diodes
Carbon quantum dots
Modulation effect
Performance improvement

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CQDs (the inset is the HRTEM image of CQDs). (b) PL spectra (solid lines) and absorption spectrum (dashed line) of the CQDs.
(a) Schematic structure of WOLED device 1. (b) Schematic structure of WOLED device 2.

(a) Current density-voltage (J-V) curves of WOLEDs. (b) Luminance-voltage (L-V) curves of the WOLEDs. (c) Enhanced electron - hole pairs (excitons) multiplication in CQDs (From Reference 26.) (d) Energy band schematics of the device 2.

Characteristics of WOLED devices. (a) Electroluminescent spectra of the device 1 under different forward voltage. (b) Electroluminescent spectra of the device 2 under different forward voltage. (c) CIE chromaticity coordinates of device 1. (d) CIE chromaticity coordinates of device 2. (*the insets in panel (c) and (d) are the Photographs of WOLED devices).
(a) The schematic structure of YOLED device 3. (b) The schematic structure of YOLED device 4.
(a) Current density-voltage (J-V) curves of the YOLEDs. (b) Energy band gap schematics of device 4.

Characteristics of YOLED devices. (a) Electroluminescent spectra of the device 3 under different forward voltage. (b) Electroluminescent spectra of the device 4 under different forward voltage. (c) CIE chromaticity coordinates of device 3. (d) CIE chromaticity coordinates of device 4.

head.ce:title: Modulation effect of carbon quantum dots in organic electroluminescent devices

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author.School of Physical Science and Technology, LanZhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are embedded as a functional material into white-organic-light-emitting diodes (WOLEDs) devices with a structure of dual-light-emitting layer. When the forward voltage is applied on devices ranging from 14 V to 22 V, the CIE coordinates of CQDs WOLEDs changed from (0.27,0.29) to (0.34,0.37) which exhibits linear optical modulation effect. Meanwhile the as-fabricated CQDs WOLEDs obtain excellent optical and electrical properties. By embedding CQDs, WOLEDs with a low turn-on voltage of 12 V which is 40% lower than reference devices can be achieved. Moreover, the devices could also obtain high luminous brightness with a lower voltage. Such superior properties are attributed to the CQDs multiple exciton generation (MEG) effect. The results demonstrate that CQDs as functional materials of WOLEDs may open up new thoughts to develop the optically modifiable WOLEDs and the low power consumption optoelectronic devices.

ce:section.ce:para: White organic-light-emitting diodes (WOLEDs) have attracted widespread attention in recent years due to theirs high efficiency, high brightness and low energy loss [1–3]. WOLEDs can achieve full-color display by using a filter membrane technique [4]. However, white light goes through a filter will exhibit a very large loss. Therefore, the WOLED devices which can be optics-modulated exhibit a huge advantage in the display and lighting field.

Quantum dots (QDs) have become one of the ideal active materials for OLED and other optoelectronic devices because of their advantageous characteristics of high quality crystal structure, adjustable band-gap and others excellent quantum effect [5–11]. Carbon quantum dot (CQD) is a nanoparticle consists of a carbonaceous skeleton with a surface group whose size is less than 10 nm. A lot of works for using CQDs as the light-emitting materials of OLED devices were reported because of the excellent optical properties of CQDs, such as luminous stability, superior resistance to photobleaching, upconversion fluorescence emission characteristic and electrochemical luminescence property [10,12–22]. CQDs can also exhibit different luminous colors in the different external conditions with a certain degree of optical modulation capability [20]. But there is a very serious constraint of CQDs based OLED devices that it is difficult to obtain a high luminous intensity (at 102 orders of magnitude) due to the fluorescence quenching phenomenon caused by CQD particles aggregation [17–22]. The studies that CQDs are used as the light-emitting layer of OLED devices are difficult to further develop due to CQDs fluorescence quenching phenomenon. However, the fluorescence emitting by CQDs can be effectively quenched by an electron acceptor or electron donor, so the light-excited CQDs are both excellent electron acceptors and electron donors [23–25]. This light-induced electron transfer mechanism of CQDs can be applied to photoelectric conversion in optoelectronic devices. At the same time, QDs have multiple exciton generation (MEG) effect that QDs could produce multiple excitons by an excited photon [26,27]. Therefore, in this work we no longer use CQDs as the light-emitting materials in OLED devices but use CQDs as enhancement materials of the devices to enhance the photoelectric performance.

In this work, CQDs were prepared by microwave-assisted hydrothermal method of bottom-up method [10,12,28–30], and the as-obtained CQDs were embedded into a WOLED device with dual-light-emitting layer structure to form a mosaic structure. Experimental results show the CQDs WOLEDs have achieve excellent optical modulation effect. Unlike the optical modulation effect of OLED devices that alter the emission peaks of the devices light emitting layer of the devices, the CQDs WOLEDs achieve the optical modulation effect by using the generated excitons from CQDs to adjust the luminous chromaticity proportion of the WOLEDs. Since CQDs do not emit light in devices, the fluorescence quenching of CQDs can be avoided. In the previous reports, there were rare studies that CQDs were used as functional materials rather than as luminescent materials in optically modifiable OLED devices. And that compared with the original WOLED device, besides optical modulation effect, the CQDs WOLEDs can also obtain excellent optical and electrical properties. CQDs WOLEDs exhibit a higher current density (Increased by 60%), a lower turn-on voltage (reduced by 40%), and achieve high luminous brightness with a lower voltage. This work demonstrates the superior spectral modulation effect and high photoelectric performance of the WOLEDs using CQDs as functional materials.

The CQDs were prepared by a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method [10,12,28–30]. 3 g citric acid and urea by the mass ratio of 1:2 were added into 20 ml deionized water. The mixture was stirred by a magnetic stirring apparatus until it formed a transparent solution. Then the transparent solution was heated in a microwave oven for about 5 min to get a dark clustered solid sample. After that, the solid sample was placed into a vacuum drying oven 1 h at 80 °C to remove residual small molecule compounds. Then the solid sample was dissolved in 100 ml anhydrous ethanol to get CQDs saturated solution.
In order to compare and analyze the impacts of CQDs in OLED devices, four groups of devices were fabricated (see Table 1 ).

NPB [N, N-diphenyl-N, N-bis(1-naphthyl) -(1,1-biphenyl)-4,4-diamine] and Alq3 [tris(8-hydroxyquinoline)-aluminium] were used as the hole-transport layer (HTL) and electron-transport layer (ETL) because of their superior hole and electron transporting capability with mobility of about 1.1 × 10−5-3.5 × 10−4 cm2 V−1 s−1 and 4.63–5.43 cm2 V−1 s−1, respectively. MADN [2-methyl-9,10-di (2-naphthyl)-anthracene]: TBPe (1,4,7,10-Tetra (tert-butyl) perylene) and MADN: Rubrene (5,6,11,12-tetraphe-nylnaphthacene) were served as blue light-emitting layer (EML) and yellow light-emitting layer. MADN was used as the host of TBPe and Rubrene. m-MTDATA (4,4′,4″-tris [phenyl(3-methylphenyl) amino] triphenylamine) was used as the hole inject layer (HIL). And BCP (2,9-dimethyl-4,7-diphenyl-1,10-phenanthroline) was used as blocking layer. The mentioned organic materials above were purchased from Aldrich Chemical Co. Ltd. with the purity of more than 99%. The fabrications of OLED devices were by a vacuum thermal evaporation method. And the saturated ethanol solution of CQDs were spun-coated onto MADN: TBPe (12 wt%) layer.

The current density-voltage J-V characteristics of devices were measured by using a Keithley source-meter (model 2410). Electroluminescence (EL) spectra luminance and the CIE coordinates were measured by a Photo Research SSP3112-JCH Spectral Tester. The evaporated organic materials film thickness was measured by a quartz monitor crystal. All the measurements of devices were carried out at room temperature.

The surface structural and morphological characterizations of CQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM). Fig. 1 (a) is the TEM image of CQDs which shows that the size of the as-fabricated CQDs are around 2–5 nm. A nearly granular distribution without too much aggregation is shown in image. HRTEM images (the inset) showed the prepared CQDs were distributed with diameters in the range of 4–5 nm with discernible lattice structures.

Fig. 1(b) shows the UV–Vis absorption spectrum (dashed line) and photoluminescence (PL) fluorescence emission spectra (solid lines) of CQDs. From Fig. 1(b), we can see that there are two obvious absorption peaks at 360 nm and 420 nm, respectively. It is found that it shows an excitation-wavelength-dependent effect of CQDs. That is, as the motivated wavelength of the UV–Vis source changed, the fluorescence emission wavelength of the CQDs changed correspondingly. It is also support that CQDs can be used as light-emitting materials for optical modulation. The maximal emission peak of PL spectra is 530 nm which was obtained at exciting-wave length of 420 nm. When the motivated wavelength of UV–Vis source was ranged from 360 nm to 450 nm, the fluorescence emissions peaks of CQDs were almost at 530 nm which is green fluorescence emission.

Schematic structures of WOLEDs are shown in Fig. 2 . Fig. 2(a) is the schematic structure of the original WOLED device (device 1) without CQDs. Fig. 2(b) is the schematic structure of the contrastive WOLED device (device 2) that the CQDs were embedded into MADN: Rubrene layer. Other structural parameters of WOLEDs are identical. On the basis of the distribution and size of the CQDs are shown in Fig. 1, it is known that the CQDs are distributed on the surface of the device with the form of dispersed particles. Thus, the prepared MADN: Rubrene film layer and CQDs form a compact mosaic structure.

Fig. 3 (a) shows the J-V curves of the WOLEDs. As shown by the J-V curves, the current density of device 2 increases dramatically as the driven voltage increases. And the current density of the device 2 at a driving voltage of 20 V can achieve twice than the current density of the device 1 at a driving voltage of 30 V. The turn-on voltage of device 2 is about 12 V, which is 40% lower than the turn-on voltage of 20 V of device 1. That is, by embedding CQDs into device 2 results in a significant enhancement in the electrical performance of device 2.

The excellent electrical performance of device 2 is speculated to be a consequence of MEG effect of CQDs. As is well-known, when a device only is inserted with some organic layers, because the increase of the device thickness, it almost leads to reducing of current density of device [31]. However, the current density of device 2 has been increased when the CQDs are inserted into device 2. It is an opposite result to above phenomenon. Therefore, the great possibility of this phenomenon is that CQDs produce a large number of carriers at the excitation voltage. When a semiconductor is in the bulk phase state, the energy level is a series of continuous level. However, when a semiconductor is in the quantum state, because of the three-dimensional quantum limited effect in quantum dots, the energy level position will be divided. That is, the energy level of the quantum state semiconductor will be in a discrete state [26]. The energy level schematic of CQDs is shown in Fig. 3(c). Compared with the bulk phase graphite carbon, the energy level of the CQDs is a series of discrete levels, which allows the CQDs to absorb different energy excitons selectively. Meanwhile, the formation of the divided energy level of CQDs can effectively decrease the electric phonons interactions, which lead to the confinement effect of carriers and the reinforcement coulomb effect of electron-hole will be enhanced. Therefore, the rate of Auger processes and the inverse Auger process of exciton multiplication is greatly enhanced [27]. A hot electron excitation can not only produce the electron-hole pair but also produce more and more second electron-hole pairs. In fact, this phenomenon is multiple exciton generation (MEG) effect of quantum dots which is also shown in Fig. 3(c). Thus, due to the MEG effect of the CQDs, when the CQDs are embedded into device 2, CQDs generate a large number of carriers under the excitation of the forward voltage of the OLED. These carriers are drifted under the electric field action and injected into the light emitting layer of the OLED device, that make the turn-on voltage of the device reduced and the carrier density of the device improved greatly. Comparing with device 1, CQDs WOLED has a higher electrical efficiency. It means MEG effect of CQDs are also enhancing other performances of WOLEDs.

The luminance-voltage (L-V) curves of WOLEDs were plotted as shown in Fig. 3(b). It reflects the optical performance of the OLED devices. All the optical data are from the SSP3112-JCH Spectral Tester. A maximum luminance 19500 cd/m2 is obtained at 19 V of device 2, which is obviously higher than a maximum luminance 14600 cd/m2 of device 1 at 30 V by comparing the optical properties of the two sets of devices. It is illustrating that the CQDs OLED can achieve high light-brightness under low voltage, which exhibit superior optical performance. According to the MEG effect of the CQDs as previous description, we already know that CQDs can generate multiple carriers by an exciton, but we do not know the direct qualitative relationship between the carriers generated in CQDs and the external excitation. Therefore, we could only quantitatively analyze the relationship between the carriers generated in CQDs and luminous brightness through the L-V curves of devices. Fig. 3(d) shows the energy band schematics of the device 2, The energy level of CQDs were measured by electrochemical cyclic voltammetry [13,28]. The HOMO (highest occupied molecular orbital) and LUMO (lowest unoccupied molecular orbital) energy levels of the CQDs were estimated to be −3.9 eV and −5.5 eV, respectively. From Fig. 3(d) we can see that there is almost no hole injection barrier between CQDs and Rubrene. On the contrary, there is a high electron barrier between CQDs and NPB. Therefore, the holes generated in the CQDs can be easily injected into the Rubrene, and the electrons generated in the CQDs can hardly be injected into the TBPe molecules. Comparing with device 1, the device 2 is lost relatively a part of the blue light-emitting during the luminescence process, which should lead to reduce device luminous brightness. However, device 2 can achieve greater brightness at lower voltages. That is, the excess carriers generated in the CQDs compensate for the luminous brightness of the devices is more than the loss of the luminous brightness of the devices. It proves that the holes injected into the Rubrene can be efficiently compounded due to the excellent hole recombination ability of the Rubrene. Therefore, the introduction of CQD can greatly improve the luminous efficiency of the device.

Fig. 4 (a) is the normalized EL spectra of device 1. It shows three emission peaks of device 1 at 460 nm, 560 nm and 600 nm, respectively. 460 nm emission peak originate from TBPe emission. 560 nm and 600 nm emission peaks originate from Rubrene emission. As the voltage increases, the emission peak of Rubrene also increases slightly. According to the absorption spectra of Rubrene, it exhibits three absorption peaks at 456 nm, 490 nm and 526 nm, respectively. Thus, a part of photons from TBPe can be absorbed by Rubrene layer. However, this effect in device 1 is very weak. The CIE coordinates image of device 1 (Fig. 4(c)) exhibits all the coordinates points distributing in white light region are (0.32, 0.35), (0.32, 0.36), (0.32, 0.36), (0.33, 0.37) and (0.33, 0.37), respectively, with the voltage increasing from 25 V to 30 V.

Fig. 4(b) is the normalized EL spectra of device 2. The main emission peaks of device 2 are also at 460 nm, 560 nm and 600 nm, respectively. And the emission peaks of Rubrene 560 nm and 600 nm also increases correspondingly with the increase of voltage. When the forward voltage is applied to the device ranging from 14 V to 22 V, the CIE coordinates of device 2 (Fig. 4(d)) moving to (0.34,0.37) from (0.27,0.29) linearly. Device 2 exhibits a strong stable luminous chromaticity modulation effect that is suitable for solid-state lighting applications. This phenomenon of device 2 must be caused by the introduction of CQDs. Comparing the EL spectra of the device 2 with the PL spectra of CQDs, we find that there is no corresponding emission peak of CQDs in EL spectra of devices. Therefore, there is no fluorescence emission of CQDs when the device 2 exhibit red-shift phenomenon. It is known from Fig. 3(d) that the carriers generated in the CQDs can be injected into the Rubrene molecules instead of the TBPe molecules. And because of the mosaic structure between CQDs and Rubrene, Rubrene also have a part contact with TBPe, which could inject a part of carriers into TBPe [31]. According to the previous analysis of the optical properties of the device 2, it lost a portion of the blue light emission compared to the reference device 1. However, the brightness of the device 2 is greatly increased. That is, the injected carriers make the yellow luminescence intensity increase of Rubrene which is much higher than the blue luminescence intensity increase of TBPe, and results in an increase in the total brightness of the device 2. Meanwhile, as the voltage increases, the proportion of yellow light-emitting in the white light-emitting gradually increases. The chromaticity of the device gradually changes. We believe that CQDs WOLEDs with an optical modulation effect will achieve full color display finally, which have a huge advantage in the display and lighting field.

Another aspect should be considered is that CQDs could combine with other organic molecules to form an exciplex, which will create a new emission peak in the OLED spectrum. In order to exclude the possibility which changing the chromaticity of WOLED due to the formation of composite molecules by CQDs with Rubrene, another experiment has been designed. The schematic structure of yellow organic-light-emitting diodes (YOLEDs) is shown in Fig. 5 . Comparing with WOLEDs, TBPe layer wasn't fabricated in the YOLEDs. Similarly, the only difference between device 3 (original YOLED) and device 4 (contrastive YOLED) is whether CQDs were introduced in.

Fig. 6 (a) shows the current density-voltage (J-V) curves of the YOLEDs. Comparing with device 3, device 4 still has a lower turn-on voltage which is reduced by 35% and a high current density. The J-V curves demonstrate that the MEG effect of CQDs is still applicable in such YOLEDs. Similar to device 2, CQDs and Rubrene also form a mosaic structure in device 4. Fig. 6(b) is the energy band schematics of device 4. It shows that there is a high electronic barrier between CQDs and NPB which leads to electron injection blocking. That is, the hole-injected Rubrene molecules in the CQDs cause the increase of the current density in device.

EL normalized spectra of device 3 are shown in Fig. 7 (a), in which it can be seen that two emission peaks appear at 560 nm and 600 nm, respectively. All two emission peaks come from Rubrene light-emitting. Similar to device 3, the emission peaks of the spectrum of device 4 are also at 560 nm and 600 nm respectively, which are shown in Fig. 7(b). As the voltage increases, the EL spectra of device 3 and device 4 both exhibit a rather small variation. And the CIE coordinates of device 3 and device 4 (Fig. 7(c) and (d)) are also located in the similar to the same location (0.50,0.48). CQDs based YOLEDs shows a very high stability at the applied voltage. That is, with increases of the forward voltage, the EL spectrum of device 4 do not obtain new emission peak. Thus, it can be inferred that CQDs and Rubrene hasn't formed exciplex in the OLED devices. It can be ruled out that the change of the chromaticity of WOLED (device 2) is caused by the CQDs and Rubrene form exciplex luminescence.

Notably, we found that the EL spectra of device 4 exhibit a stronger intensity at 600 nm wavelength emission than that of device 3. This is also the reason that the CIE coordinates of the two sets of devices are not in the same location. According to the previously mentioned theory of the MEG effect of CQDs, due to the presence of the split energy levels, the CQDs can generate multiple carriers through an exciton, but the energy of the generated carriers is not same. With the increase in the number of multi-excitons generation, the generated carrier energy is gradually decreasing. So, the carriers with relatively low energy are absorbed by the Rubrene molecules and excite the light with a long wavelength and a relatively low energy, which makes the devices on the carriers utilization greatly improved. Therefore, the intensity of the EL spectra of the device 4 at the wavelength of 600 nm is stronger than that of the device 3. That is, the MEG effect of CQDs can also optically modulate such structure YOLEDs.

In this study, CQDs are used as functional materials of OLED and make CQDs and Rubrene forming a mosaic structure. By embedding CQDs into WOLED devices, the photoelectric performances of the devices have been significantly prompted. As the voltage increases, the CQDs WOLED exhibits a linear luminescence change. Meanwhile, the turn-on voltage of the CQDs WOLEDs is reduced by 40%. The maximum luminous brightness of devices is also significantly suggestive. In another group of yellow OLED devices preparation experiments, the turn-on voltage of CQDs YOLEDs is reduced by 35%. And the carriers' utilization of the devices are also greatly improved. All the experimental results demonstrate that CQDs have a very good modulation effect on the OLED optical and electrical characteristics. CQDs as functional materials of WOLEDs may open up new thoughts on the full-color, low power consumption and high efficiency OLED devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Provincial Natural Science Foundation of Gansu (No. 1606RJZA026), Scientific and Technological Project of Chengguan District of Lanzhou (No. 2016-2-4) and get funding by Institute of Sensor Technology, Gansu Academy of Science.
